input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab-scale gantry crane system consisting of physical crane and controller components, operating in a laboratory environment for research and educational purposes. Key characteristics include public availability, open-source nature, and reliance on freely available common software to ensure reproducibility for digital twin studies.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services for optimal control of the gantry crane system, historical data logging of operational parameters, real-time data visualization for system monitoring, and continuous validation against the physical system's behavior. These services are targeted at researchers and educators to validate digital twin methodologies such as synchronization methods, improved data streaming, and system evolution notations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The system utilizes Python for core implementation, Docker for containerized deployment, TimescaleDB as a time-series database for historical data storage, Grafana for interactive data visualization dashboards, and Eclipse Mosquitto as an MQTT broker for real-time data streaming between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system's architecture is structured using the C4 Model, with context and container diagrams defining component interactions and system organization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin utilizes CAD models for geometric representation and a kinematic model for motion simulation of the gantry crane. Simulation services support trajectory planning and validation, while TimescaleDB serves as the data management system, storing all generated operational data for real-time analysis and model updates.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Air Quality Management exemplar features a Raspberry Pi with sensors for indoor air quality monitoring; however, specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, sampling frequencies, and technical specifications are not detailed in the provided documentation.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Continuous validation is performed by comparing executed trajectory measurements against simulations using Euclidean distance metrics; discrepancies exceeding threshold values trigger error correction processes to maintain model fidelity and system accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from the physical crane to the digital twin via the Physical Crane Interface, a Python program connecting to the crane's controllers to capture real-time operational state data (e.g., position, velocity). The Logging Application continuously streams this data to a TimescaleDB database, ensuring real-time synchronization. Transmission occurs continuously during operation with no specified frequency, and data quality is maintained through direct sensor integration. Preprocessing steps and communication protocols beyond the Python interface are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Trajectory Generator Service generates optimal movement trajectories using a kinematic model of the crane, which are processed by the Control Application. The Control Application uses the Physical Crane Interface (Python program) to send command signals (e.g., trajectory instructions) to the physical crane's controllers. Validation occurs via the Continuous Validation Service comparing simulated and executed trajectories, though command message formats and authentication mechanisms are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components run on a single personal computer interconnected via an MQTT-based network topology using Eclipse Mosquitto as the broker for inter-service communication (e.g., Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service). The physical crane is connected directly to the computer via a hardware interface (e.g., USB/serial), though specifics are not detailed. Bandwidth, latency, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployed on-premise on a single personal computer with TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT Broker, and Grafana Dashboard containerized using Docker via a docker-compose file. Other components (Control Application, Logging Application) run as Python applications on the host machine. Scalability and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed, but the deployment supports single-node execution with containerized services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Lab-Scale Gantry Crane system utilizes the C4 Model for architecture visualization through context and container diagrams, and employs an ad-hoc development methodology without a commercial digital twin framework, incorporating containerization technologies for code dissemination.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Lab-Scale Gantry Crane provides services including data logging, data visualization, optimal trajectory generation for puck movement, and continuous validation to support operational decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Lab-Scale Gantry Crane follows an ad-hoc approach without adherence to specific industry standards or frameworks, utilizing containerization for deployment but not following formal standardization protocols.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

The description accurately lists the specific services provided and correctly identifies the target users and research methodologies. It is well-supported by the text in the Abstract and Introduction. Quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'demonstrator for... synchronization methods... data streaming... DarTwin, a notation for system evolution'.

The extraction precisely lists the technologies used (Python, Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, Eclipse Mosquitto) and correctly describes their specific roles within the system architecture and implementation.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

The description states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly details the physical actuators and controllers. It lists 'Two Motors and associated Controllers' and 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist'.

The extraction describes the 'Air Quality Management' exemplar mentioned in the background section, which is irrelevant to the specific system under study (the Gantry Crane). Provides a specific and accurate description of the continuous validation process, including the comparison of executed trajectories versus simulations and the threshold-based notification system. block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 'Physical Crane Interface' as a Python program connecting to controllers and the 'Logging Application' streaming data to 'TimescaleDB'. It accurately notes the continuous nature of the logging. However, it substitutes the specific sensor data types mentioned in the source (swing motion, wind speed) with generic terms like 'position, velocity', missing some key technical details present in the component diagram. Supported by quotes: 'Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database', 'Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist'.

Accurately describes the control flow from the 'Trajectory Generator Service' through the 'Control Application' to the 'Physical Crane Interface'. It correctly identifies the purpose of the 'Continuous Validation Service' and the specific mechanism of comparing simulated and executed trajectories. The exclusion of message formats and authentication is correct as they are not in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories', 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane', 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories'.

Highly specific and grounded in the source. It correctly identifies the deployment on a single personal computer, the use of 'Eclipse Mosquitto' as the MQTT broker for inter-service communication, and the direct connection of the physical crane. It correctly notes the absence of QoS details like bandwidth and latency in the text. Supported by quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer', 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services', 'Eclipse Mosquitto was used'.

Excellent extraction that captures the hybrid deployment model: containerized services (TimescaleDB, MQTT Broker, Grafana) vs. host applications (Python modules like Control and Logging). It accurately specifies the on-premise, single-node deployment on a personal computer and the use of a docker-compose file. Supported by quotes: 'plug in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of the software on that computer', 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)

4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
